speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a coat, and you're warm, and it's-- you're having a hell of a good time. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you start out this time, chronologically give me an idea how old you are now. You're-- you're alone now, and how old do you think you were? What year was this? When you come back--

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean in-- in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you re-slate him, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Wait a second. No, no, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: You should always do this [INAUDIBLE] before the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just read it. Just tell us where you are.

words.text: read it. Just tell us where you

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Henry Meyer, November 20, 1995, Cincinnati, Ohio, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Henry Wolfgang Meyer, November 20, 1995. Interviewer is Susan [? Harkavy. ?] The city is Cincinnati. The country is United States, and the language is English. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, if we can just go back to your job at Auschwitz, and you were cleaning the-- the SS doctors' barracks. And what years was this approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must be 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was too good to last. And I don't know what did it that it suddenly changed. Somehow, when I got into the camp, I must have mentioned that I am a musician. And there were bands in practically every concentration camp. Obviously, the Germans can't do a damn thing. Well, let's put it-- the Nazis can't do a damn thing without music. So I was requested to join the band in Birkenau. Auschwitz was the main camp. Birkenau was the camp where the four crematories were lined up. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Auschwitz had stone barracks. Birkenau had wooden barracks for horses, and there I was. Had to leave my jacket, everything. Got back into an old prison outfit and was sent do Birkenau. There, I got a violin in my hand, and I had to play. Now, I don't know what I played and how I played, but the conductor wasn't very much of a musician either. He just thought I was wonderful, and I immediately was in the band. I got dressed a little bit better. That was one of the privileges. And we had to play in the morning for the commandos, which are the working teams, leaving the camp, and in the evening when they came back. The rest of the day we had to work. But there were certain moments where we had to entertain the SS. And that meant before commandos went out in the morning, we played popular tunes. And we played potpourris. We played waltzes, tangos, whatever was known. Then we played march music, and then perhaps we played another. And then we went back to our work. I'll speak about the work later. And in the evening was the same procedure. After appell at night, members of the orchestra split up into little combos and entertained certain blockalteste, chief of barracks, and certain SS who came to listen to us at night into the camp. I did not take part in that too much because I was not trained in the jazz field, and I didn't improvise very well on the violin. And-- so I-- once in a while I-- I did something. I learned it over the years.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I was a member of the marching band. Now have you ever seen a violinist in a marching band? This wasn't very good. And with the attitude of these people of saying, why don't you play? Who needs you? Out! You know, I could have lost my situation, my-- my good position-- even better position than others. So I must have said that somewhere before, I'm born under a lucky star. The cymbal player of the band got sick. And I went to the conductor and said, I am an absolute virtuoso on the cymbals-- have never having had a cymbal in my hand. He said, so you play.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I played as everybody would do with a cymbal, bang these things together, and nearly broke my wrist. Because, you see, the air becomes steel, so to say. You-- you strike the cymbals. You don't bang them. Well, I did learn that fast, and I became a very popular star. The SS guards would throw stones-- thrown my way, and I had to catch them in time on the cymbals and things like that. All little tricks. Once in a while, they also hit somebody from the band. You know. [INAUDIBLE]. Well, the life of the band. Now, as we were also there for the entertainment of the SS, they made the most of it. We were invited to play the crematories. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This brutal officer in charge liked music. And whenever there was a Sunday, he would get us there. We played in the facilities where other people were gassed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was told to us, well, aren't you a bit afraid in there? And the only thing which kept us not being too afraid to play was that they were with us in there, the SS guys. If we would have been alone, I don't know what we'd have done. So you actually played inside the crematoria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. What did you see in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you saw-- it looked like a-- a-- a shower room. And it said on the walls, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- one lice, your death, and all kinds of good slogans to keep clean. And then, of course, that was all faked. It was just put in. There was never a drop of water in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, these were one of the activities. There's another activity, which I have to report. I've never forgotten. We found out the favorite songs of certain SS characters. Either they brought them from home and said, could you play that for us? And we would. Or they were popular tunes, and we knew that they liked it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well there was one guy who was from Berlin, and he liked the tune which was called, 'Berliner Luft,' 'The Air of Berlin.' And the procedure was so the minute you saw somebody who like the tune, we broke up what we played and the conductor would say, let's play 'Berliner Luft' you know, when this guy appeared. So this guy appeared, and the conductor said, 'Berliner Luft.' We played 'Berliner Luft.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the SS guy? No, I think I didn't-- never knew. What we overlooked and didn't see, was just at that moment, the Sonderkommando, the special commander who worked in the crematories and who stayed in a special barrack secluded from us in camp. And of course, and was-- was not wearing a-- prison clothes but civil-- civil clothes. And they had always a terrible smell about them. You know those guys-- they-- they were so-- their clothes took the smell, and everybody knew. These were the guys from the crematoria. They were hardly passed when the guy-- guy came furious. Stop! Put the instruments away. So we put the instruments away. [GERMAN]. Now [GERMAN] was something where you were doing all kinds of exercises, if possible, in the dirt, when it was raining. And the SS would be in between either beating the hell out of you at the same time or stepping on you, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So very often-- really very often-- one of the band didn't survive it. At that moment, this particular time, two Greek members died. They were just not able to do it. They beat the hell out of them, and that was the end of them. Much, much later, the conductor asked this SS guy, and said, but officer, why were you so furious? We were trying to do you a favor and play you, and you-- he said, what? With the Sonderkommando walking out, you play 'Berliner Luft'? It had never occurred to us. Of course, I mean, if you have a bad conscience, you react that way. That's what I have never forgotten. The--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in the band for quite awhile, and then the Russians appeared. In the-- Perhaps I should say a little bit about the-- the orchestra [? such. ?] The orchestra was made up of professional musicians in the jazz field, most of them coming from Holland. They were not all [? Hollanders, ?] but they lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, or a little bit before, the best jazz was played in England and in Holland. So the people who we got were of this great American standard of jazz. You know, Gene Krupa here? We had Morris-- Morris-- god, now at this minute I forgot the name. He was a drummer-- Morris [? Van ?] [? Clave ?]. He was absolutely sensational, and so were very many others. So what the jazz-- what the SS got were really first class players. Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were Greek people in the orchestra with whom I had little contact. I couldn't speak to them. They spoke French. They didn't speak German, or they spoke their Hebrew-- their Sephardic dialect. Ladino or something, it's called. And I have met a number of them who survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through my profession with the LaSalle String Quartet, I got all around the world. And I made-- made it a point to go and visit with some of these people. We had very good times, and I have seen a number of them. Very many have died since then. We have two or three in this country, and I have interviewed a bunch for another tape. That was the band. But I have not mentioned the work which we had to do during daytime. We got a big cart, which could be drawn by two or four horses, even. And with this one, we were pushing, about 20 people. It wasn't easy, because it was very heavy. But it permitted us to go to other camps. Now Birkenau was made up of men camp, gypsies, Theresienstadt people, and the big women's camp. And that's where we wanted to go, partly, to keep some contacts. If you, for instance-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one player, an accordion player in our band had two sisters in the orchestra, in the women's camp. I had a very good old friend. As a matter of fact, she got into it-- a little bit through my head when she was deported. And I see her still, she and her sister. In-- one is in London, the other in Paris-- in France. And we moved this car with all kinds of things. For instance, when there had to be-- every month all our blankets and everything went into the sauna to be cleaned so that lice and everything-- and then we put them back again. So we did this. But also, in the camp, there was a little bit sand, a little hill of sand. And we started to transport that sand into the women's camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For no good reason whatsoever, we just deposited some there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, it was nice. We went every day to the women's camp. We all got ourselves girlfriends, and-- and we were sort of spoiled by some of them, having our shirts washed or something. This sort of-- you know, in camps, you organize things, too. So-- then it was the end of the-- the sand.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any sand anymore. So how are we going to maintain our relationships and going there. We brought the whole sand back into our camp. Completely useless activities for about three, four months, under the noses of the SS. They never questioned what we had there. They never asked what we are transporting for them back and forth. But this was the life we were with. Anyway, it was good for us. And this was the activity of the-- the band during daytime. Well, one heard more and more that Russians were approaching. And one day, there was a blow up in one of the crematories. And shortly after that, it wasn't safe anymore to keep us there. And the Nazis wanted to eliminate the evidence, and we were transported into Germany. Now imagine, by this time the war efforts were becoming more and more difficult. The Germans were on the losing side. They had to transport their own prisoners, and so on and so on. And at the same time, they would load us into cars and move us all over the country. We went from Auschwitz to Oranienburg to Sachsenhausen. From Sachsenhausen to Buchenwald, and from Buchenwald, they opened new camps, Ohrdruf, where I was. O-H-R-D-R-U-F. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There, the prisoners [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] built the launching base for the V-2. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The second day, standing there, an SS officer from Auschwitz or Birkenau, Stiebitz-- S-T-I-E-B-I-T-Z said, [? musica. ?] [? Musica, ?] you're going to be blockalteste-- chief of a barrack. I said no. Yes, you are-- five. So I became chief of the barrack. I got very, very-- it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: My barrack never was in order. It never was right. I couldn't beat, and you needed to. You know, that's what human beings are-- in transit, you know, and don't want-- and-- and-- I don't understand it. But it was them. So who gets beaten in a case like that? The blockalteste-- me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he beat me. It was just terrible. And out of that, sometimes-- and doesn't know how this happened-- I just stood up. And I said, look, I'm going out to do work. I'm not going to be blockalteste anymore. I'm not going to be beaten to death at that time of the game-- of the game, because I knew there was, perhaps, a chance that we could. And the man came to reason and stopped, and said, no, you're not going to do that. But you are going to be the chief of barrack-- of the block one. Now that was for 30 people, and they were all big shots in the camp-- you know, lageralteste, [GERMAN]. These are all the people who have special functions in the camp. And I thought for a second, I said, all right. Now, if I listen-- if I think of this conversation today, I think perhaps I make them up. But this is actually how it was. And then I was this [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And that man visited me every evening. And one day he came and said, you know what we are going to do? I will bring you civilian clothes. You have already quite a bit of hair. You can put it a little bit different.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we'll escape, both together. And we go to the Americans, and you will tell them I'm a good man. I'm bringing you here to the Americans, and so they will spare my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stiebitz. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stiebitz, that's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't think so. Because if somebody will kill me, it's you. You have tried that now for over two years. I'm not going to be killed by the Americans because I bring a Nazi. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No. Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our camp, I read in the-- in some correspondence, that they are going to kill all of us in the transport from Ohrdruf to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you see this correspondence?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the-- in the office, in the-- and somebody was careless left a letter lying around. It was actually for Nazis, but I saw it and I knew about it. So the date came, and we were supposed to go back and-- to-- to Buchenwald. And I was standing next to this guy, and one person after the other disappeared. And I said, oh, my god, I'm going to be alone with him. And then I have to do what he wants to do or he will shoot me. Well, here comes another reminder. I said I'm born under a lucky star. I had planned with three other guys to escape if we are being transported back to Buchenwald. But there I was stuck. And my three friends were standing somewhere, and we're avoiding being let out so that they would be around. That moment, a huge Mercedes appears. In this Mercedes was the commander of Buchenwald. And he yells, Stiebitz, come here! And he ran, got into the car, drove off. I got my three colleagues. I reported to the SS, which took the numbers down. 104994 and 99 haftlinge-- prisoners-- out of the camp, and I marched out. We marched through the evening. In the evening, one of these Croatian guards who was going with us-- I had talked to him. I said, come on, take off your uniform. And if somebody comes, we will say you are one of us. No. He wasn't going to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it came evening. He said, you speak German very well. Come with me. So I went with him to one of the beer joints in the next village. And we asked where the Americans are, and they said, well, the Americans were here already last week. But they went back again, and so they're there and there and there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guy [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] said, let's have a beer. So we had the beer. And he had the beer with a cognac at the same time. You have to have that too, so I just flipped it over the counter, and it was gone. And he had another beer with that one. Pretty soon he was asleep, and I took his gun. I went out and I put it somewhere in the snow. It was already snow around there. And I went back to the guys I had left. I said, come on, we go. And we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: We must have gone no more than about five hundred feet. And we laid down, and I had a bread, which I had under my hat. I slept so deep that the next morning when I woke up, there was no more bread. So somebody had taken it, but I hadn't noticed it. What to do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it happened that this was the time when Patton was called back-- General Patton-- so that the Russians could come to Berlin at the same time. So well, there we are in the middle of nowhere, nothing to eat, nothing to drink. When it rained, we were licking the leaves and eating whatever looked green, you know. We just took it and ate something. Finally, one day we found some potatoes-- not-- what grows around it. And we were hiding it. It's a wonder we got it. There were three soldiers. One, it was not the-- the normal-- it's-- what do you call it here if the president calls for the-- the people who take over instead of soldiers. Well, anyway, they were not soldiers. They were called [NON-ENGLISH], but I don't know what the translation is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they didn't quite know what to do with us. So they spoke, and I detected immediately at the Saxonian dialect where I come from. So I said, where are you from? He said from Dresden. I said, well, so am I. And he said, so, what are you doing? Yeah, I said, my parents were [INAUDIBLE] [? Von ?] [? Meyer. ?] Oh, he said. I know that store. I have been there very often. So he did the following thing. He took out piece of paper and hold it down. Today-- with the date-- I have taken prisoners, and listed our four names.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was me, it was a-- somebody from Slovakia, from Bratislava. He died in the meantime. A Greek person, [INAUDIBLE], lives in New York. And [? Bela ?] [? Fabian, ?] or as the Hungarians say, [? Fabian ?] [? Bela. ?] He was the head of the-- of the Democratic party in-- in Hungary. He died in New York a number of years ago. Anyway, he wrote it all down. I have taken them prisoner and let them go again. And I had to sign that. Well, sort of showing himself as a good person. And he gave us four cigarettes, a blanket. And he said, if you go down there, you're running exactly into the first row of the German front lines. Good luck. And we went, and we were running into the front line. And there, things became really terrible. There was a young officer who took us prisoner again. And there was a brewery near. And he put this there, and they had cellars. And he got us down and down and down. Put us against the wall, insulted us, the dirty Jews. And [INAUDIBLE]. And you think you have gotten by. I'll kill you all. And we were standing against the wall and through the events that had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: When a voice from above comes and says, what are you doing down there? He said, I am sure shooting these dirty characters. He said, are you crazy? The Americans are upstairs. So he ran off. There I collapsed. That was the first time where I-- I was out. And I was woken up by a Dutch-- again, Dutch-- slave laborer who had put a bucket of water over me. And I woke up and I said, where are the Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, they are upstairs on the other side of the street. They will be here in a minute. Just relax. I said, nothing doing. I'm going. So I went up, and I grabbed up my three colleagues behind me, and we got out. And they were shooting. It was just terrible. And on our bellies, we crossed the street and we got to the other side-- literally on the other side of the street were the Americans. And boy, what a change.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you stop right here?
